I also connected to a free weather API and even made a simple agent using Python's OS tools to create files on your local desktop or if you run this in your vs code  , even can generate code files for your project based on instructions ( like mini cursor 😀 )

Here are some of the things I asked it:

Show me the top 3 best-selling products based on total sales amount and display the results clearly.

Result: It correctly figured out it needed to SUM the sales, GROUP by product, ORDER them, and LIMIT to 3. It generated the SQL and showed me the table!

You can even see behind the curtain in the "Execution Details" to see the SQL it wrote.

Can you show me sales of eclars  -  (Deliberate typo!)

  • Result: Success! Because we told the AI brain (in the System Prompt) to always use case-insensitive searching (UPPER()), it found the sales data for "Eclairs" even with my typo.

In Other Language : 

Muestra los tres productos más vendidos según el monto total de ventas y muestra los resultados claramente  -

Create a line chart comparing the monthly sales trend for our two key products: '85% Dark Bars' and '99% Dark & Pure'. Title it 'Monthly Sales Trend: Key Products'. – Complex Visualization

Result: This was more complex! It first ran a query to get the monthly sales for both products, then correctly called the charting agent to create a line chart with two distinct lines – perfect for visual comparison.

What was the exact total sales amount generated in the USA for August 2022 and weather of New York?

  • Result: This was cool – it seamlessly called the HANA agent for the sales figure and then called the Weather agent for NYC, presenting both pieces of information in the final answer. Two agents working together!

Negative Testing:

Show me all the data in the chocolate sales table.

Result:  The assistant blocked this! It recognized that asking for the entire table without filters is usually a bad idea. This is possible with System Prompt.

While this prototype provides a glimpse of the potential, the underlying agentic architecture is designed to be scalable and can be further developed using industry-standard practices for security, monitoring, and robustness.

So, what does this all mean? By blending the conversational Power of an LLM with focused agents that can securely talk to our real systems (like HANA, APIs, or other tools), we're building more than just a Q&A bot.

We're creating tools that feel natural, breaking down technical barriers and putting powerful data insights directly into more people's hands. It feels like we're just scratching the surface – I truly envision a future where this kind of seamless AI integration becomes the norm, making all our tools smarter, friendlier, and ultimately, more helpful.

Github link : 

https://github.com/abhimanyu-agi/genAI--SAP-HANA-AI-Assistant

Demo Video:

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=UApzc5pQLXI

entry.title: How to migrate Boomi Integrations to SAP Integration Suite
entry.content:
1. Purpose of the document

This blog provides a comprehensive guide on migrating SAP SuccessFactors HCM integrations from Boomi to SAP Integration Suite (SAP IS), highlighting its strategic advantages, including prepackaged integrations, Tech Envo Accelerators, Migration Framework, Migration Process,  Design considerations, and hybrid deployment options. It covers SAP’s runtime environments, deployment models, and TechEnvo’s migration accelerators, ensuring a seamless migration. With TechEnvo’s expertise and adherence to SAP best practices, organizations can modernize their integration landscape while ensuring scalability, consistency, and long-term success.

 

2. Why SAP SuccessFactors HCM customer needs to migrate from Boomi to SAP Integration Suite

  • Between 2013 and 2016, SAP SuccessFactors HCM customers used BOOMI as an integration platform to support data integration requirements in SAP SuccessFactors HCM implementation, and many customers are still running on Boomi.
  • SAP Customers have significantly invested in SAP BTP and SAP Integration Suite in the last seven years as part of S4HANA transitions and Integration Modernization (moving from SAP PO to SAP IS). 
  • Customers are migrating interfaces from Boomi to the SAP Integration Suite, aligning with the SAP Integration strategy.
  • Maintaining multiple integration platforms adds a lot of Technical Debt, and SAP customers consolidate and move to one integration platform (SAP Integration Suite) 
  • SAP Integration Suite Capabilities on par with BOOMI

3. SAP Integration Suite Capabilities and Features

SAP Integration Suite (SAP IS) is the strategic integration platform for SAP customers, offering seamless connectivity with over 3,400 prebuilt integrations and 3,800+ APIs. It provides all the capabilities of Boomi and additional advantages such as prepackaged content covering most of the SAP Solutions Integrations, AI-driven integration, and enhanced security. 

SAP Integration Suite is a comprehensive set of tools and services that enable seamless integration across various SAP and third-party systems. It helps businesses connect, automate, and optimize their processes across cloud and on-premises environments. Here are some key features and components of the SAP Integration Suite:

3.1 Key Features:

  • Connectivity: Provides pre-built connectors and adapters for integrating various SAP and third-party applications, such as SAP S/4HANA, SAP SuccessFactors, and external applications like Salesforce and Microsoft Dynamics.
  • Integration Flows: Facilitates the orchestration of data between applications with customizable integration flows, enabling businesses to automate workflows and synchronize data.
  • API Management: Allows businesses to manage their APIs in a secure and scalable manner, with tools to create, expose, and govern APIs across multiple environments.
  • Data Transformation: Supports the transformation of data between different formats (e.g., XML, JSON) to ensure seamless communication between systems.
  • Monitoring and Analytics: Provides dashboards and monitoring tools for real-time visibility into integration processes and system health, allowing proactive management.
  • Cloud-Based: Being cloud-native, SAP Integration Suite provides flexibility and scalability to handle evolving integration needs.
  • Security: Offers robust security features, including authentication, authorization, encryption, and secure communication for data exchanges

3.2 SAP Integration Suite Core Components:

  1. SAP Cloud Integration (CPI): A cloud-based integration platform for connecting SAP and non-SAP applications. It allows seamless integration of cloud and on-premise systems.
  2. SAP API Management: A solution to design, publish, and monitor APIs, providing tools to securely expose and consume APIs.
  3. SAP Open Connectors: A collection of pre-built connectors to a variety of third-party applications and services.
  4. SAP Integration Advisor: A tool that automates the mapping and transformation of data across systems, helping streamline the integration process.
  5. SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP): While the Integration Suite is a part of BTP, it works closely with other BTP services like SAP HANA, SAP AI, and SAP analytics for a comprehensive digital transformation solution.

3.3 SAP Integration Suite Use Cases:

  • ERP Integration: Connect SAP SuccessFactors HCM and SAP S/4HANA with other enterprise systems, such as CRM, Payroll, and Time, Benefits..etc, to ensure a unified view of business operations.
  • Cloud and On-Premises Integration: Link cloud-based applications (e.g., SAP SuccessFactors HCM ) with on-premises systems (e.g., SAP ECC).
  • API-First Strategy: Enable businesses to expose services as APIs and integrate them with external systems for broader connectivity.
  • Data Synchronization: Automatically sync data between systems, ensuring consistency and eliminating manual data entry errors.

3.4 SAP Integration Suite Benefits:

  • Faster Time-to-Value: Pre-built integrations and connectors reduce implementation time.
  • Scalability: Easily scale integration scenarios as your business grows and systems evolve.
  • Flexibility: The suite supports various integration scenarios, from simple point-to-point integrations to complex, multi-cloud architectures.
  • Cost Efficiency: Reduces integration complexity and costs by leveraging a unified platform for all integration needs.

In summary, SAP Integration Suite enables organizations to drive digital transformation by ensuring seamless integration across their entire IT landscape, whether using SAP or third-party applications.

 

4.Boomi to SAP Integration Suite Migration – Tech Envo Framework 

We have worked on multiple Boomi to SAP Integration Suite migration projects and developed a framework and strategy for successful migrations.

 

4.1 Scope of Boomi Integrations to be migrated to SAP Integration Suite:

This is the first step in the migration process: identifying the number of integrations currently running in the Boomi Production Atom environment and ensuring the scope is determined. 

4.2 Document pain points and current environment: 

Talk to the business and technical team supporting the integrations in the BOOMI environment and get a list of pain points and challenges. This is a critical step, and addressing these issues in the SAP Integration Suite after migration is necessary.

Common pain points : 

  1. Poor Integration design 
  2. Poor Integration coding standards 
  3. Not implementing prepackaged integrations
  4. Customizing prepackaged integrations 
  5. Heavy Groovy scripting
  6. Lack of better error handling mechanisms
  7. Lack of documentation
  8. Not using the correct SAP SuccessFactors APIs
  9. Delta Logic is missing 
  10. No encryption
  11. Authentication type issues 
  12. Tightly coupled interfaces - not easy to make updates.

4.3 Identify Integration Patterns: 

Identity integration patterns like Synchronous, Asynchronous, cloud-to-cloud, cloud-to-on-premises, On-premise—on-premise Integration, Scheduled, Real-Time, Type of Systems involved, etc., and create an SAP Integration Suite Integration patterns covering all scenarios. 

  • Synchronous Integration – Real-time data exchange with immediate response—API-to-API communication between SAP SuccessFactors and another system.


                   Fig: Payments canada to SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central Master Data Sync Integration

Fig: High Resolution flow of Payments canada to SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central Master Data Sync

  • Asynchronous Integration – Data is sent without waiting for an immediate response.Sending employee updates from SAP SuccessFactors to External Applications.

Fig: Integration Flow of SAP SFEC to Metlife Employee Data Sync.

Fig:  High Resolution flow of SAP SFEC to Metlife Employee Data Sync Integration.

  • Cloud-to-Cloud Integration – Data exchange between two cloud applications.Syncing data between SAP SuccessFactors and another cloud-based system(ServiceNow,Okta, Workday,SAP Concur, SAP Fieldglass, S4HANA Cloud.etc)

Fig: Integration Flow of SAP SuccessFactors Onboarding to OKTA Data Sync Integration

Fig: High Resolution Flowof SAP SuccessFactors Onboarding to OKTA Data Sync Integration

  • Cloud-to-On-Premises Integration – Communication between a cloud-based system and an on-premises system.Triggering updates in an on-premises system from a cloud-based application(S4 HANA OP, SAP ECC, Data Lake OP, SFTP OP).

Fig: Integration Flow of SAP SFEC to ShareWorks Employee Data Sync

Fig: High Resolution Flow of SAP SFEC to ShareWorks Employee Data Sync

  • On-Premises to On-Premises Integration – Data exchange between two on-premises systems.Sending employee updates from SAP SuccessFactors to External Applications.

Fig:  Integration Flow of SAP S4HANA or SAP ECC to BankMaster Data Replication

Fig: High Resolution Flow of SAP S4HANA or SAP ECC to BankMaster Data Replication

  • Scheduled Integration – Batch processing executed at defined intervals.Daily batch transfer of employee records from SAP SuccessFactors.

Fig: Integration Flow of SAP SuccessFactors Onboarding to Qualtrics Data Sync 

Fig: High Resolution flow of SAP SuccessFactors Onboarding to Qualtrics Data Sync 

  • Real-Time Integration – Event-driven or API-based real-time integration.Triggering real-time updates in one system based on events in another.
  • Employee Demographic Data Outbound Integrations

Fig: Active directory(Outbound) to SAP SuccessFactors Employee Demographic Data Sync

  • Employee Data Update Inbound Integrations

Fig: Integration Flow of Active directory(Inbound) to SAP SuccessFactors Employee Demographic Data Sync

Fig: High Resolution Flow Active directory(Inbound) to SAP SuccessFactors Employee Demographic Data Sync

4.4 Create Migration Strategy 

  • Create a detailed strategy covering all aspects of migration:
  1. Deployment Approach and Timelines
  2. Prioritization of interfaces
  3. Technical Architecture
  4. Documenting best practices 

4.5 Map Boomi capabilities with SAP Integration Suite

Identify capabilities used in BOOMI and map which capabilities you need to use in IS. This will give a clear picture of gaps in capabilities and how they can be solved, such as connectors used, API management, Scripting, security, Retry mechanism, error logging capabilities, etc. 

Tech Envo's best practices always include using standard capabilities offered by the SAP integration suite and implementation prepacked integration before building custom integrations.  

4.6 Create Technical Architecture / Design and Development standards

Creating a technical design for each interface is critical. The technical design can be reused depending on the integration type and pattern. Create detailed technical design standards and a framework for the topics below. 

  • Development standards
  • Naming conventions
  • Reusable framework / a common framework (reuse of scripts, security materials, etc)
  • Documentation standards and deliverables
  • Interface development life cycle
  • Interface testing 
  • API best practices 

4.7 Integration Build and Testing

Ensure that the integration consultant working on the integration build has the expertise below. This will help build high-quality integrations. 

  • A clear understanding of SAP SuccessFactors HCM APIs 
  • Excellent hands-on experience in the SAP Integration suite 
  • Experience in Integration build for SAP SuccessFactors HCM
  • Able to understand BOOMI integration code (Boomi platform)

Create a detailed development process for all integrations, align on the development approach, and implement leading practices. This will ensure consultants working on the project follow the same standards and help support the interfaces better.

For example, if you are building delta logic for SAP SuccessFactors HCM data extract, ensure this logic can be reusable across interfaces. Usually, we create a reusable Groovy script. Maintaining the code would be less effort if everyone used the same approach.

4.8 Integrations Testing and comparing BOOMI/SAP Integration Suite Outputs. 

Integration testing is a critical phase of the project. Ensure you document all test cases, check if any are available from the Boomi project, and enhance the same with additional cases. Run the integration in the Boomi environment and SAP Integration suite and compare data. If data matches both platforms, logic integration in SAP integration suits and covers all requirements but follows this process for all scenarios. 

4.9 Deployment in SAP Integration Suite Production 

Once the business approves the integration, plan the deployment in the SAP Integration Suite production environment and schedule as required. Un-deploy or stop the Boomi schedule for the interfaces deployed in the SAP IS prod environment. 

5. Boomi to SAP Cloud Integration - Migration High-Level Decision Flow to determine Integration approach 

The primary goal of the below decision is to leverage prepackaged integrations as much as possible before building a custom integration solution. This approach aligns with the recommendation of SAP's leading practices and SAP clean core principles.

 

 

6. Tech Envo Migration Accelerators

TechEnvo Migration Accelerators include 74+ prepackaged integrations and templates for SAP SuccessFactors HXM in SAP Integration Suite, reusable Framework, and an advanced error-handling framework. Our team is experts in Boomi, SAP Integration Suite, and SAP SuccessFactors HCM solutions, with deep knowledge of SAP SuccessFactors APIs and best practices. With 700+ integrations built using SAP Integration Suite and Boomi, we bring extensive migration experience, ensuring a seamless transition from Boomi to SAP Integration Suite. Our certified professionals have successfully executed multiple Boomi-to-SAP IS migration projects, leveraging industry-leading methodologies.

non SAP ERP applicaitons

TE73

SAP SuccessFactors EC to Oracle NetSuite Emp Data Sync

SAP SuccessFactors EC

 

TE74

S4 HANA to SAP SuccessFactors RCM Job Req Int

SAP S4 HANA

SF RCM

7. Tech Envo Migration Framework  – SAP SuccessFactors Integration Approach

As part of the migration, 

We develop high-quality integrations in SAP Integration Suite, utilizing its full capabilities to ensure optimal performance. Our approach prioritizes leveraging pre-delivered features with minimal customization by using standard mapping functions and predefined process steps offered by SAP Integration Suite. We also enforce industry-leading development standards aligned with SAP best practices and organizations' leading practices, ensuring consistency, maintainability, and scalability across all integrations.

 

  1. We implement direct API-based Direct integrations if the vendor has built integration and is available in the SAP App Center (e.g., Hire Right, Nakisa, JDMS, SailPoint, Omada IDM, Okta, etc.). Direct integrations avoid the middleware layer and reduce development and support costs.
  2. We implement prepackaged integrations developed by SAP, Tech Envo, or Vendor to reduce development effort and customization.
  3.  We use SAP SuccessFactors Integration Center (IC) if the Integration Center integrations require less effort to build and maintain.
  4. We have built reusable integration templates, which we can use if we need to develop a custom integration. 

 

8. SAP Integration Suite Technical Landscape, Cloud Foundry Environment

  • Cloud Foundry: The Cloud Foundry environment in SAP BTP is an open-source application runtime supporting Java, Node.js, and other languages, ideal for extending SAP products, integrating applications, and building enterprise solutions using SAP-hosted APIs. 
  • https://help.sap.com/docs/cloud-integration/sap-cloud-integration/technical-landscape-cloud-foundry-environment 

 

9. Deployment Environments of SAP Integration Suite

  • Cloud Integration Runtime: A cloud-based service in SAP Integration Suite, enabling seamless data exchange between cloud and on-premise applications like Boomi Cloud  Atoms.
  • Edge Integration Cell: A hybrid integration runtime in SAP Integration Suite that allows designing in the cloud while running integrations on-premise, equivalent to Boomi On-Premise Atom
  • https://help.sap.com/docs/integration-suite/sap-integration-suite/setting-up-and-managing-edge-integration-cell 
  • Hybrid Deployment Option: Customer can use both Cloud Integration Runtime and Edge Integration Cell - Hybrid deployment option. 

 

10. SFEC to SAP ERP/S4HANA Integrations Migration Approach

We migrate Boomi legacy SAP S4 HANA, SAP ERP HCM, SAP ECC integrations to the BIB Replication Framework for seamless. Employee and Organizational Data replication. Our expertise includes implementing BIB-based integrations for multiple customers, including SAP S/4HANA environments. We have developed accelerators to handle complex replication scenarios, such as dynamic actions, pensioners’ data replication, concurrent employment, and global assignments, ensuring a robust and efficient migration process and no impact to payroll during migration.

 

Migration Key Considerations

1.Connectivity

  • The IP address changes for  Boomi data center (Atoms) and SAP IS are different, so the firewall ports and network config must be updated with new IP range details during migration.
  • Server and Client certificates in the on-premise ERP system must also be updated during migration.
  • Any custom integrations built using SuccessFactors SMTP server for sending emails must be changed to a publicly available SMTP server, as SuccessFactors does not allow external access to its SMTP server.
  • Any locally installed atom needs to be changed to use SAP Cloud Connector (SCC)/Edge Integration. SAP Cloud Connector connects on-premise systems with SAP IS without opening tunnels.

2.Instance Strategy:

  • Depending on the license model, SAP provides two tenants (Test and Production) by default, but we recommend using a three-tier architecture.
  • Multi-tier architecture requires an additional SAP IS tenant, which may involve extra costs.

 

3.Cloud to On-Premise Connectivity

  • On-premise connections via Boomi Local Atom need migration to SAP Cloud Connector or Edge Integration Cell.
  • Proxy tools used in Boomi can still be leveraged in SAP Cloud Integration.

 

4.Interface Mapping: Existing interfaces need to be evaluated based on the following 4 buckets:

  1. Boomi Pre-Packaged to SAP IS Pre-Packaged /Direct API-based integration 
  2. Boomi custom integration to SAP IS a Pre-Packaged integration 
  3. Boomi Custom Integration to SF Integration Center Integrations
  4. Custom Boomi to Custom SAP Integration Suite 

5.Interface Prioritization

  1. Prioritize high-risk integrations such as Payroll, Time, and Benefits 
  2. Ensure SAP IS generates output with the same logic as Boomi.
  3. Validate output files between Boomi and SAP IS to streamline testing.

entry.title: Welcome to Season 9 of SAP Let's Talk Data: Building Trust in the Data-Driven World
entry.content:
Hello, data enthusiasts and business leaders! We're thrilled to announce the kick-off of Season 9 of the SAP Let's Talk Data podcast series. This season, we're diving deep into the real challenges of data today and how building trust in data can transform your business.

Season 9 Video 

Why Trust in Data Matters

We live in a data-driven world where businesses are investing billions into AI, analytics, and automation. However, there’s a significant hurdle—many companies still don’t trust their data. Without this trust, AI can’t deliver, automation stalls, and digital transformation never reaches its full potential.

That’s why we created SAP Let’s Talk Data—the podcast that tackles the real challenges of data today. Trusted, business-ready data isn’t just important; it’s the foundation for everything else. Trust in data doesn’t happen by accident. It’s built through strong governance, secure AI, and the ability to connect all your data in meaningful ways. When done right, trusted data empowers businesses to model scenarios, drive innovation, and unlock the full value of AI.

What’s in Store for Season 9?

This season is packed with insights, case studies, and practical tips from data practitioners and innovators. Here are some of the exciting episodes that are lined up:

  • 📌The Evolution and Impact of AI in eSports: How SAP’s BTP Keeps Team Liquid One Step Ahead - Discover how AI and analytics are transforming eSports, providing strategic insights and boosting performance.
  • 📌The Future of Enterprise AI starts with Knowledge Graphs, Powered by SAP HANA Cloud - Dive deep into what knowledge graphs are, how they transform data analysis, and the value they unlock for organizations already using HANA Cloud or considering it. 
  • 📌 Accelerate transformation with a focus on data management and modernization – Learn how data preparation and transformation can help companies reduce costs, accelerate time to value, and minimize risk during transformation projects.
  • 📌Transforming Supply Chain Management - Explore AI-powered search and optimization techniques that are revolutionizing logistics.
  • 📌SAP Business Data Cloud - Understand how contextualized, reliable, and accessible, this data becomes the fuel for advanced 360-degree or AI-driven insights. 
  • 📌Unlocking Business Data for AI-Powered Applications - Delve into the strategies for building next-gen, data-intensive apps that are context-aware and securely connected.

Join the Conversation

Welcome to SAP Let’s Talk Data, where we break down how businesses can go from data overload to data confidence—unlocking AI, smarter decision-making, and real transformation.  We’re excited to embark on this journey with you. Whether you’re a seasoned data professional or new to the field, there’s something for everyone in Season 9 of Let’s Talk Data. Stay tuned, and let’s build a future where every piece of data is trusted and reliable.

Subscribe Now!

Don’t miss out on our insightful discussions and expert opinions. Subscribe to SAP Let’s Talk Data on:

  • Spotify
  • Apple Podcasts
  • YouTube
  • And more…

New episodes are live now, so get ready to dive deep into the world of data. Let’s talk data!

Interested in more SAP podcasts? 

Check out the SAP BTPTalk series to keep up to data with what’s new in SAP Business Technology Platform.

entry.title: SAP Sapphire Orlando 2025: An Architect’s guide: SAP Business Data Cloud and SAP Databricks - BDC263
entry.content:
Hi Everyone, My name is Gaurish Dessai and I’m a Principal Enterprise Architect at SAP. I am part of Global Enterprise Architecture Advisory team which works closely with our customers and on strategic projects. I am a hands-on Enterprise Architect, with over 13+ years of experience in Architecting and delivering programs from idea to adoption. I bring strong expertise in Supply chain, Sales, Digital, M&A, Divestiture, Sustainability & Manufacturing from both customer and consulting background.

My Session: An architect’s guide: SAP Business Data Cloud and SAP Databricks solutions - BDC2633. I am presenting together with Michelle Wiktorsson (Databricks) on 20th May 2025 at 1:00 PM - 1:40 PM EDT as part of the SAP Sapphire ASUG Pre-conference in Orlando.

I couldn’t be more excited about this! I started working in the SAP world as a junior SAP Sales & Distribution (SD) functional consultant in 2012. We were a bunch of fresh campus-hire graduates and received 2 months of intense SAP functional and technical training at the learning academy in Hyderabad.

As an junior SAP ‘resource’ working in support and projects from an offshore development center (ODC) for a very large client, attending SAP Sapphire was a distant dream! SAP Sapphire was when the whole ODC would be buzzing about latest launches by SAP for days and followed up by reading about them on SAP Community blog

Since then, 13 SAP Sapphire’s later, the moment came! I got confirmation of our session being selected for SAP Sapphire ASUG Pre-Conference at Orlando, I was over the moon.  It was actually my co-presenter, Michelle's idea to propose a join session on the topic of SAP Business Data Cloud  and Databricks tailored for Architects – and I am glad that we followed through with that idea.

So, here we are, less than 20 days as I write this post and we submit the first draft of our session for review with the ASUG team! In our session, we would be talking about aspects of SAP Business Data Cloud and Databricks which are critical for every architect to know.

What will you learn from the session?

  1. Why SAP Business Data Cloud and Why Now?
  2. What does it mean for architects
  3. What are the capabilities of SAP Business Data and Databricks
  4. Transition paths to modernizing your Data landscape
  5. Architects role in making trusted data available to the business
  6. How Architects can derive value from SAP BDC and Databricks

So, If you’re an architect navigating the evolving data landscape—or just curious about how SAP BDC and Databricks, feel free to drop into our session. Michelle and I are bringing together practical insights, real-world patterns, and a good bit of our combined experience to make it worth your time. Hope to see you there and geek out over data and architecture with us!

Now let’s hear from you - please feel free to share your thoughts in the comments! 🚀 

Please answer these question/s by adding a comment in the comments section (e.g. 1-B,2-C):   

Question 1:   “Which capabilities of SAP Business Data Cloud would you like to understand in detail?”  

Multiple Choice:  

  1. A) Enterprise-wide data integration across SAP and non-SAP environments  
  2. B) Advanced data governance, compliance, and security management  
  3. C) Embedded AI, machine learning, and intelligent analytics capabilities  

Question 2:   “As you prepare your data landscape for AI, which areas are your highest priorities?”  

Multiple Choice:  

  1. A) Driving data quality, consistency, and accessibility at scale  
  2. B) Building a scalable, resilient infrastructure for AI and ML workloads  
  3. C) Strengthening data governance frameworks to enable responsible AI  

Related community tags (Subscribe to remain current): SAP Business Data Cloud 

Related community topic pages: SAP Business Data Cloud 

Link to our session on the SAP Sapphire Session Catalog- https://www.sap.com/events/sapphire/flow/sap/so25/catalog-inperson/page/catalog/session/1742305886714001DI6M

entry.title: Transition of Replication services from SAP HANA Cockpit to SAP HANA Cloud Central
entry.content:
HANA Cloud Replication Services enable real-time or scheduled (snapshot) data replication from various source systems into SAP HANA Cloud. These services are designed to ensure high data availability, consistency, and low-latency access by synchronizing data between operational and analytical environments. Replication is done via Smart Data Access (SDA) and can be set up for replicating from HANA on-premise databases to HANA Cloud databases.

SAP HANA Cockpit provided the platform for managing replication services. While this service has been widely used, now it is being phasing out from HANA Cockpit in favor of SAP HANA Cloud Central. SAP HANA Cloud Central is the unified management platform for all SAP HANA Cloud services, including replication. Moving forward, this platform will provide the functionalities for managing replication tasks and improve user experience.

The below are the steps of creating data replication using the SAP HANA Cloud Central.

  • Step 1: Log in to SAP BTP Cockpit. Navigate to Instances and Subscriptions. Launch the SAP HANA Cloud tools (SAP HANA Cloud Central) application. If it is not present, kindly subscribe for the same by selecting it from the Create button of Instances and Subscriptions page.

  • Step 2: Select (click) the SAP HANA Cloud Database in which the data replication needs to be configured.

  • Step 3: Once the SAP HANA Cloud Database is selected, a right panel will open with Overview of the selected SAP HANA Cloud Database.

  • Step 4: Inside the panel, search for the Data Replication tile and click on Create Replication Task button.

  • Step 5: Under Connection Type, '+ Create a New Connection' will be selected by default. Depending on the source, select the required connection type and click on Next Step.
    Please note that 'SAP HANA 2.0 On-Premise SPS 05 or Higher' is selected here for illustration purposes.

  • Step 6: Provide an appropriate Connection Name, Host along with its Port, and SAP Cloud Connector Location ID. To ensure secure communication, enable SSL Encryption. Also, enter a Technical User from the source system that has the following privileges, along with its Password. Click on Next Step once details are provided.
    • CATALOG READ.
    • SELECT on the schemas of the users to be replicated.
    • SELECT on schemas with objects to be replicated.
    • SELECT on any other objects to be replicated.

  • Step 7: Upon entering valid details, a Green tick with "Connection (TEST_REPLICATION) has been created" message will appear.
    Optional Step: Select the users if it needs to be replicated to the SAP HANA Cloud database and for this, specify whether to allow or disallow the replication of privileges. For each replicated user, a new password needs to be set. Post this, Click on Next Step.

  • Step 8: Now, select the objects (Table / View) for which replication needs to be enabled. Use the search bar for getting the required objects. Choose the replication schedule as "Real Time" for real-time data replication or "None" for data snapshots. Once the objects are selected, click Confirm.

  • Step 9: By clicking the Confirm button, the replication will be configured and triggered. Once the tables are replicated, the status for Real Time will show as "Loading..." and the status for None (Snapshot) will show as "Completed." For snapshot replication, you can schedule it based the requirement by clicking the "Set Replication Schedule" button. If there are multiple objects, the overall replication status can be seen on the left-hand side.

  • Step 10: Cross-verify all the details by opening the SAP HANA Database Explorer and perform the following check.
    • The connection name provided initially will appear under Remote Sources.
    • The replicated tables will appear under Tables with the name VT_CONNECTIONNAME_SCHEMANAME_TABLENAME. Select it and click on Open Data to view the replicated table data.

entry.title: Sapphire Orlando&Madrid 2025: World champion analytics and performance in esports with SAP software
entry.content:
Hi SAP Community, My name is Joschua Goetz and I'm an IT Technology Services Senior Specialist, working in a very special project about esports. Together with our Partner Team Liquid we build esports analytics dashboards to boost team and player performance.

I’m excited to participate at SAP Sapphire Orlando and Madrid this year, where I’ll be joined by Team Liquid to talk about our awesome partnership and how we use analytics to help them win.

My Sessions Overview

Orlando:
World champion analytics and performance in esports with SAP software - DIS2157 

Here i have the honor to present together with Thom Valks Partnerships Manager at Team Liquid. Our session is at 3:30 PM - 3:50 PM EDT on May 20. 

Madrid:
World champion analytics and performance in esports with SAP software - DIS1454 

In Madrid i'll be on stage with Jason Lucas Luijckx Director of Partnerships at Team Liquid. Our session is scheduled at 11:30 AM - 11:50 AM CEST on May 28.

What you will learn about

The thrilling intersection of esports and innovative technology as you join us to experience the transformative power of SAP software in competitive gaming. Learn how our internal IT helps leading esports organization Team Liquid with SAP Analytics Cloud, SAP AI Core, Joule, and SAP Business Technology Platform.

Looking forward to seeing you

The world of esports is a data rich, high stakes world of competitive gaming. With SAP software it is possible to gain that ever so small edge over your opponents. Curious how we did it with Team Liquid ?

Register here for our SAP Sapphire sessions in Orlando or Madrid

Not able to attend SAP Sapphire but still curious ? Learn more about our partnership here SAP x Team Liquid 

Now let’s hear from you

Did you know that SAP was involved in esports ? 
If you play DOTA2 or League of Legends, what do you think could be some of the most influential parts of the game we should look at ?
Share your thoughts in the comments.

entry.title: Initializing Replication from SAP ASE to SAP HDLRE using Bulk Materialization
entry.content:
This article provides step-by-step instructions for setting up a real-time replication environment from SAP Adaptive Server Enterprise (ASE) On Premise to SAP HANA Cloud data lake Relational Engine (HDLRE) in a BTP subaccount, SAP Replication Server (SRS) and Cloud Connector.  Subscriptions are initialized using the bulk materialization capability to HDLRE introduced in SAP Replication Server 16.1 (released 2025-02-28).

Using bulk materialization allows existing data in the source ASE database to be materialized to HDLRE as part of the subscription definition, eliminating the need for a separate extraction and load step or starting with empty source tables.

Other than bulk materialization, the steps described are similar to those documented in Replicating from SAP Adaptive Server Enterprise to SAP HANA Cloud Datalake Relational Engine except as follows:

  • The target HDLRE will be defined in non CF-enabled subaccount (including the srs proxy connection service required).
  • A non-administrative HDLRE account will be defined as the table owner HDLRE and DSI connection from SRS.

Full database replication is not possible as this is heterogenous replication.  Table level replication is used to replicate required tables.  Table level replication is described in documentation (Create an SAP Replication Server On-Premise Remote Server | SAP Help Portal). 

Using table level replication allows finer control over database replication as not all tables may need to be replicated.

 

1. Install, create and configure an ASE On Premise

ASE 16.1 is installed in the $SYBASE folder (Adaptive Server Enterprise/16.1 SP00 PL00/EBF 30672 SMP/P/x86_64/Linux 5.14.21-150400.24.147-default/ase161sp00pl00/3343/64-bit/FBO/Thu Feb 6 02:47:09 2025).  Instance SAMPLE_ASE is running on port 5000.

An empty 'tpcc' database (~6GB) will be used as the starting point on the ASE.

 

2. Install, create and configure an SRS On Premise.

SRS 16.1 is installed in its own $SYBASE path (Replication Server/16.1/EBF 30761 SP00 PL00 rs161sp00pl00/Linux AMD64/Linux 5.14.21-150400.24.136-default x86_64/3251/OPT64/Sun Dec 29 10:05:13 2024). 

Create a SAMPLE_RS instance using $SYBASE/$SYBASE_REP/install/rs_init with a partition size of 4GB.

Once the SRS instance is created, run the following configuration commands:

configure replication server set dsi_bulk_copy to "off"
configure replication server set dynamic_sql to "off"
configure replication server set memory_limit to "16384

Parallel DSI's cannot be used for HDLRE as loading must be via a single connection, but catch up queues will be automatically created for bulk materialization.

 

3. Install and Start Cloud Connector On Premise

Download and install the latest cloud connector from https://tools.hana.ondemand.com/#cloud.  As of this writing, the current version is 2.18.0.  For this blog, the Linux portable version will be used but the daemon version can be installed and run instead, if required.

Cloud connector requires a newer JRE.  SAP JRE can be downloaded from https://sap.github.io/SapMachine/.  For this writing, SAP JVM 21 (21.0.7) will be used and JAVA_HOME set accordingly.

Once installed, start the Cloud Connector.

Cloud Connector documentation can be found at: https://help.sap.com/docs/connectivity/sap-btp-connectivity-cf/cloud-connector.  If this is the first time connecting to Cloud Connector, several proscribed steps must be taken to connect as 'Administrator' and change default passwords.

 

4. Connect to BTP Cockpit

Connect to SAP BTP Cockpit (e.g. https://amer.cockpit.btp.cloud.sap) and access the sub account from the 'Overview' where the HDLRE instance will be created. 

Locate and copy the BTP subaccount id (see screenshot below):

 

5. Configure Cloud Connector

Connect to Cloud Connector (e.g. http://<cloudconnectorhost>:8443 ) and configure a new subaccount using the subaccount number from above.  Fill in the proxy details if necessary and choose 'Configure manually'.  Fill in the dialog similar to the following using a valid BTP subaccount userid and password:

Note: if using MFA via an authenticator app for the login, append the time-generated code to the end of the password.

Once added, select 'Cloud To On Premise' on the left menu under 'Replication Demo' and fill in the prompts of the dialogs as follows to add the Virtual to Internal System mapping:

  • Backend Type: Other SAP System
  • Protocol: TCP
  • Internal host: <name of CC host from 'hostname' shell command>
  • Port or Port Range: <port on which SRS is listening - from $SYBASE/interfaces file> (e.g. 11752)
  • Virtual Host: <SRS name from interfaces file> (e.g. SAMPLE_RS)
  • Virtual Port: <same port as above>

On the 'Summary' dialog, ensure that 'Check Internal Host' is selected before pressing 'Finish'.  If there are any issues, corrections can be made from the presented list (example below):

 

6. Create Proxy Service in BTP

Before creating the HDLRE instance, a proxy service must be created in the BTP sub account.

Connect to the BTP subaccount, navigate to 'Serivces' -> 'Instances and Subscriptions'.  Create a new service instance using the below dialog example and select 'Create'.

Once created, select the '...' (Actions) menu and 'Create Service Binding'.

Use 'sample_rs' for the Binding Name and create the binding.

When the binding has been created, click on the link in the 'Credentials' column of the service and copy the JSON to use in the next step.

 

7. Create HDLRE instance in BTP

SAP HANA Cloud Central must be used to create the HDLRE instance.  From BTP Cockpit, install (if necessary) 'SAP HANA Cloud' application from the 'tools' plan and 'Go to Application'.

Once connected and running SAP HANA Cloud Central, create a new HDLRE instance of type 'SAP HANA Cloud, Data Lake' in the 'Other Environments' runtime environment.

  • Instance Name: HDLRE
  • Allowed connections: Allow all IP addresses
  • Enable data lake Relational Engine: yes
  • Set and confirm HDLADMIN password
  • Size: 2 VCPUs for each of the coordinator and workers
  • Enable Cloud Connector: yes
  • Service key: (paste the previously copied service binding JSON into the text box)
  • Allowed connections: Allow all IP addresses
  • Mode: Configure to be most compatible with SAP IQ

Leave the remaining details and press 'Create'.

Once created, copy the coordinator SQL Endpoint for this instance to the clipboard using the '...' (Actions) -> 'Copy Other' menu for the instance:

This endpoint contains the FQDN and port (usually 443) of the instance coordinator.  This information is used in several of the next steps.

 

8.  Add the HDLRE and ASE endpoints to the SRS interfaces file.

Edit the $SYBASE/interfaces file for the SRS instance and add details for connections to the ASE and HDLRE.  Linux example:

SAMPLE_RS
   master tcp ether <SRS instance host> 11752
   query tcp ether <SRS instance host> 11752

SAMPLE_RS_ERSSD
   master tcp ether localhost 11751
   query tcp ether localhost 11751

SAMPLE_ASE
   master tcp ether <ASE instance host> 5000
   query tcp ether <ASE instance host> 5000

HDLRE
   master tcp ether <FQDN from coordinator SQL Endpoint> 443 ssl="CN=hanacloud.ondemand.com"
   query tcp ether <FQDN from coordinator SQL Endpoint> 443 ssl="CN=hanacloud.ondemand.com"

 

9. Add the SRS endpoint to the ASE interfaces file.

Edit the $SYBASE/interfaces file for the ASE instance and add details for connections to the SRS. 

Example:

SAMPLE_ASE
   master tcp ether <ASE instance host> 5000
   query tcp ether <ASE instance host> 5000

SAMPLE_ASE_BS
   master tcp ether <ASE instance host> 5001
   query tcp ether <ASE instance host> 5001

SAMPLE_ASE_JSAGENT
   master tcp ether <ASE instance host> 4900
   query tcp ether <ASE instance host> 4900

SAMPLE_ASE_XP
   master tcp ether <ASE instance host>5002
   query tcp ether <ASE instance host>5002

SAMPLE_RS
   master tcp ether <SRS instance host> 11752
   query tcp ether <SRS instance host> 11752

 

10. Create the database and tables in ASE.

Connect to the ASE database using isql or DBISQL.  After creating the database, run the script below in the database to create the tables, columns, indexes and RI contraints:

set quoted_identifier on
go

/*==============================================================*/
/* Table: CUSTOMER                                              */
/*==============================================================*/
create table CUSTOMER (
   C_ID                 int            default '0' not null,
   C_D_ID               smallint       default '0' not null,
   C_W_ID               smallint       default '0' not null,
   C_FIRST              varchar(16)                    null,
   C_MIDDLE             char(2)                        null,
   C_LAST               varchar(16)                    null,
   C_STREET_1           varchar(20)                    null,
   C_STREET_2           varchar(20)                    null,
   C_CITY               varchar(20)                    null,
   C_STATE              char(2)                        null,
   C_ZIP                char(9)                        null,
   C_PHONE              char(16)                       null,
   C_SINCE              DATETIME     default getdate() null,
   C_CREDIT             char(2)                        null,
   C_CREDIT_LIM         decimal(12,2)                  null,
   C_DISCOUNT           decimal(4,4)                   null,
   C_BALANCE            decimal(12,2)                  null,
   C_YTD_PAYMENT        decimal(12,2)                  null,
   C_PAYMENT_CNT        int                            null,
   C_DELIVERY_CNT       int                            null,
   C_DATA               varchar(500)                   null,
   constraint C_PRIMARY primary key (C_W_ID, C_D_ID, C_ID)
)
lock datarows
go

/*==============================================================*/
/* Index: C_C_ID                                                */
/*==============================================================*/
create index C_C_ID on CUSTOMER (
C_ID ASC
)
go

/*==============================================================*/
/* Index: C_D_ID                                                */
/*==============================================================*/
create index C_D_ID on CUSTOMER (
C_D_ID ASC
)
go

/*==============================================================*/
/* Index: C_W_ID                                                */
/*==============================================================*/
create index C_W_ID on CUSTOMER (
C_W_ID ASC
)
go

/*==============================================================*/
/* Table: DISTRICT                                              */
/*==============================================================*/
create table DISTRICT (
   D_ID                 smallint       default '0' not null,
   D_W_ID               smallint       default '0' not null,
   D_NAME               varchar(10)                    null,
   D_STREET_1           varchar(20)                    null,
   D_STREET_2           varchar(20)                    null,
   D_CITY               varchar(20)                    null,
   D_STATE              char(2)                        null,
   D_ZIP                char(9)                        null,
   D_TAX                decimal(4,4)                   null,
   D_YTD                decimal(12,2)                  null,
   D_NEXT_O_ID          int                            null,
   constraint D_PRIMARY primary key (D_W_ID, D_ID)
)
lock datarows
go

/*==============================================================*/
/* Index: D_D_ID                                                */
/*==============================================================*/
create index D_D_ID on DISTRICT (
D_ID ASC
)
go

/*==============================================================*/
/* Index: D_W_ID                                                */
/*==============================================================*/
create index D_W_ID on DISTRICT (
D_W_ID ASC
)
go

/*==============================================================*/
/* Table: HISTORY                                               */
/*==============================================================*/
create table HISTORY (
   H_C_ID               int                            null,
   H_C_D_ID             smallint                       null,
   H_C_W_ID             smallint                       null,
   H_D_ID               smallint                       null,
   H_W_ID               smallint           default '0' null,
   H_DATE               DATETIME     default getdate() null,
   H_AMOUNT             decimal(6,2)                   null,
   H_DATA               varchar(24)                    null
)
lock datarows
go

/*==============================================================*/
/* Table: ITEM                                                  */
/*==============================================================*/
create table ITEM (
   I_ID                 int            default '0' not null,
   I_IM_ID              int                            null,
   I_NAME               varchar(24)                    null,
   I_PRICE              decimal(5,2)                   null,
   I_DATA               varchar(50)                    null,
   constraint PK_ITEM primary key (I_ID)
)
lock datarows
go

/*==============================================================*/
/* Table: NEW_ORDER                                             */
/*==============================================================*/
create table NEW_ORDER (
   NO_O_ID              int            default '0' not null,
   NO_D_ID              smallint       default '0' not null,
   NO_W_ID              smallint       default '0' not null,

alter table CUSTOMER
   add constraint C_FKEY_D foreign key (C_W_ID, C_D_ID)
      references DISTRICT (D_W_ID, D_ID)
go

alter table DISTRICT
   add constraint D_FKEY_W foreign key (D_W_ID)
      references WAREHOUSE (W_ID)
go

alter table HISTORY
   add constraint H_FKEY_C foreign key (H_C_W_ID, H_C_D_ID, H_C_ID)
      references CUSTOMER (C_W_ID, C_D_ID, C_ID)
go

alter table HISTORY
   add constraint H_FKEY_D foreign key (H_W_ID, H_D_ID)
      references DISTRICT (D_W_ID, D_ID)
go

alter table NEW_ORDER
   add constraint NO_FKEY_O foreign key (NO_W_ID, NO_D_ID, NO_O_ID)
      references "ORDER" (O_W_ID, O_D_ID, O_ID)
go

alter table "ORDER"
   add constraint O_FKEY_C foreign key (O_W_ID, O_D_ID, O_C_ID)
      references CUSTOMER (C_W_ID, C_D_ID, C_ID)
go

alter table ORDER_LINE
   add constraint OL_FKEY_O foreign key (OL_W_ID, OL_D_ID, OL_O_ID)
      references "ORDER" (O_W_ID, O_D_ID, O_ID)
go
alter table ORDER_LINE
   add constraint OL_FKEY_S foreign key (OL_SUPPLY_W_ID, OL_I_ID)
      references STOCK (S_W_ID, S_I_ID)
go

alter table STOCK
   add constraint S_FKEY_I foreign key (S_I_ID)
      references ITEM (I_ID)
go

alter table STOCK
   add constraint S_FKEY_W foreign key (S_W_ID)
      references WAREHOUSE (W_ID)
go

 

11. Create remote connection to SRS from HDLRE

Connect to the HDLRE endpoint using the HDLRE client using the Identification parameters:

  • Authentication: Database
  • User ID: HDLADMIN
  • Password: <password created in step 7>
  • Action: Connect to Data Lake IQ
  • Instance ID: <Instance ID from coordinator SQL Endpoint FQDN>
  • Landscape: <remainder of FQDN without Instance ID>

or use the SQL Console of SAP HANA Cloud Central directly from the HDLRE instance.

(the following steps are documented at Create an SAP Replication Server On-Premise Remote Server | SAP Help Portal.  Where differences exist, please follow the documentation).

Run the following SQL in the HDLRE console to create the connection to SRS required for replication to HDLRE:

CREATE SERVER SAMPLE_RS class 'ASEODBC' 
    USING 'Host=sample_rs:11752;
    LocationID=repdemo;
    UseCloudConnector=ON';

 

(The 'LocationID' is the value created in step 5.  The 'Host' is the virtual host and port defined in step 5).

Create a new HDLRE login:

create user tpcc identified by <password>;
grant create table to tpcc;
grant create procedure to tpcc;

Map the login for HDLRE to use with SRS:

create externlogin tpcc to SAMPLE_RS
  remote login sa identified by '<SRS sa login password>';

 

12. Create the tables in HDLRE

Reconnect to the HDLRE SQL console using the new login created above.  Run the following script to create the tables, columns and indexes (no RI is defined in this script to allow only specific tables to be replicated if desired):

/*==============================================================*/
/* Table: CUSTOMER                                              */
/*==============================================================*/
create table CUSTOMER (
   C_ID                 integer                        not null default '0',
   C_D_ID               smallint                       not null default '0',
   C_W_ID               smallint                       not null default '0',
   C_FIRST              varchar(16)                    null,
   C_MIDDLE             char(2)                        null,
   C_LAST               varchar(16)                    null,
   C_STREET_1           varchar(20)                    null,
   C_STREET_2           varchar(20)                    null,
   C_CITY               varchar(20)                    null,
   C_STATE              char(2)                        null,
   C_ZIP                char(9)                        null,
   C_PHONE              char(16)                       null,
   C_SINCE              timestamp                      null default 'getdate()',
   C_CREDIT             char(2)                        null,
   C_CREDIT_LIM         decimal(12,2)                  null,
   C_DISCOUNT           decimal(4,4)                   null,
   C_BALANCE            decimal(12,2)                  null,
   C_YTD_PAYMENT        decimal(12,2)                  null,
   C_PAYMENT_CNT        integer                        null,
   C_DELIVERY_CNT       integer                        null,
   C_DATA               varchar(500)                   null,
   constraint C_PRIMARY primary key (C_W_ID, C_D_ID, C_ID) );

/*==============================================================*/
/* Index: C_C_ID                                                */
/*==============================================================*/
create HG index C_C_ID on CUSTOMER (
C_ID
);

/*==============================================================*/
/* Index: C_D_ID                                                */
/*==============================================================*/
create HG index C_D_ID on CUSTOMER (
C_D_ID
);

/*==============================================================*/
/* Index: C_W_ID                                                */
/*==============================================================*/
create HG index C_W_ID on CUSTOMER (
C_W_ID
);

/*==============================================================*/
/* Table: DISTRICT                                              */
/*==============================================================*/
create table DISTRICT (
   D_ID                 smallint                       not null default '0',
   D_W_ID               smallint                       not null default '0',
   D_NAME               varchar(10)                    null,
   D_STREET_1           varchar(20)                    null,
   D_STREET_2           varchar(20)                    null,
   D_CITY               varchar(20)                    null,
   D_STATE              char(2)                        null,
   D_ZIP                char(9)                        null,
   D_TAX                decimal(4,4)                   null,
   D_YTD                decimal(12,2)                  null,
   D_NEXT_O_ID          integer                        null,
   constraint D_PRIMARY primary key (D_W_ID, D_ID) );

/*==============================================================*/
/* Index: D_D_ID                                                */
/*==============================================================*/
create HG index D_D_ID on DISTRICT (
D_ID
);

/*==============================================================*/
/* Table: HISTORY                                               */
/*==============================================================*/
create table HISTORY (
   H_C_ID               integer                        null,
   H_C_D_ID             smallint                       null,
   H_C_W_ID             smallint                       null,
   H_D_ID               smallint                       null,
   H_W_ID               smallint                       null default '0',
   H_DATE               timestamp                      null default 'getdate()',
   H_AMOUNT             decimal(6,2)                   null,
   H_DATA               varchar(24)                    null );

/*==============================================================*/
/* Table: ITEM                                                  */
/*==============================================================*/
create table ITEM (
   I_ID                 integer                        not null default '0',
   I_IM_ID              integer                        null,
   I_NAME               varchar(24)                    null,
   I_PRICE              decimal(5,2)                   null,
   I_DATA               varchar(50)                    null,
   constraint PK_ITEM primary key (I_ID) );

/*==============================================================*/
/* Table: NEW_ORDER                                             */
/*==============================================================*/
create table NEW_ORDER (
   NO_O_ID              integer                        not null default '0',
   NO_D_ID              smallint                       not null default '0',
   NO_W_ID              smallint                       not null default '0',
   constraint NO_PRIMARY primary key (NO_W_ID, NO_D_ID, NO_O_ID) );

/*==============================================================*/
/* Table: "ORDER"                                               */
/*==============================================================*/
create table "ORDER" (
   O_ID                 integer                        not null default '0',
   O_D_ID               smallint                       not null default '0',
   O_W_ID               smallint                       not null default '0',
   O_C_ID               integer                        null,
   O_ENTRY_D            timestamp                      null default 'getdate()',
   O_CARRIER_ID         integer                        null,
   O_OL_CNT             smallint                       null,
   O_ALL_LOCAL          smallint                       null,
   constraint O_PRIMARY primary key (O_W_ID, O_D_ID, O_ID) );

/*==============================================================*/
/* Index: O_O_ID                                                */
/*==============================================================*/
create HG index O_O_ID on "ORDER" (
O_ID
);

/*==============================================================*/
/* Index: O_D_ID                                                */
/*==============================================================*/
create HG index O_D_ID on "ORDER" (
O_D_ID
);

/*==============================================================*/
/* Index: O_W_ID                                                */
/*==============================================================*/
create HG index O_W_ID on "ORDER" (
O_W_ID
);

/*==============================================================*/
/* Index: O_C_ID                                                */
/*==============================================================*/
create HG index O_C_ID on "ORDER" (
O_C_ID
);

/*==============================================================*/
/* Table: ORDER_LINE                                            */
/*==============================================================*/
create table ORDER_LINE (
   OL_O_ID              integer                        not null default '0',
   OL_D_ID              smallint                       not null default '0',
   OL_W_ID              smallint                       not null default '0',
   OL_NUMBER            integer                        not null default '0',
   OL_I_ID              integer                        null,
   OL_SUPPLY_W_ID       smallint                       null,
   OL_DELIVERY_D        timestamp                      null,
   OL_QUANTITY          smallint                       null,
   OL_AMOUNT            decimal(6,2)                   null,
   OL_DIST_INFO         char(24)                       null,
   constraint OL_PRIMARY primary key (OL_W_ID, OL_D_ID, OL_O_ID, OL_NUMBER) );

/*==============================================================*/
/* Index: IDX1                                                  */
/*==============================================================*/
create HG index IDX1 on ORDER_LINE (
OL_W_ID,
OL_I_ID,
OL_D_ID,
OL_O_ID
);

/*==============================================================*/
